content:
3 min read
1 hour ago

--

The number on the screen said $140 for the laptop and $80 for the desktop, and the desktop had the faster chip.
I stared at that for a while. Same script, run on two machines. A Ryzen 7 5800X in a desktop I built, and a thin work laptop with a Ryzen 7 PRO 5850U. The 5800X is an 8-core desktop part that pulls over 100 watts and will happily cook a game. The 5850U is a low-power mobile chip. Any used listing would put the desktop well above the laptop. My valuation script had them backwards, and not by a rounding error.
I wrote this thing to guess what a PC would sell for. It reads the hardware, prices each part, subtracts age, adds a small bump for laptops, done. I’ve been fixing it in small pieces for a couple months, mostly around the edges. This one wasn’t an edge.
The CPU price comes from two things: a base value for the tier (a 7 is worth more than a 5) and a multiplier for how new the chip is. Both of those AMD parts are Ryzen 7, so they start from the same base. The difference had to be in the “how new” part.
It was. And the reason was dumber than I expected.
The generation detector used a regex that looked for the model number right after the tier digit. “Ryzen 7 5800X” matched cleanly: tier 7, model 5800. But “Ryzen 7 PRO 5850U” has that PRO word wedged in between, and the pattern didn’t allow for it. So the match failed, the chip came back with no detected generation, and the code fell to its catch-all multiplier.
Here is the part that made it funny instead of just wrong. That catch-all was 0.7. And the desktop 5800X, whose generation the tool did detect, was running through a completely separate bug that mapped its 5000-series number onto an Intel scale and landed it on a 0.4 multiplier meant for 2015 chips. So the laptop got 0.7 for being unrecognized, the desktop got 0.4 for being recognized wrong, and the unrecognized one won.
Two different mistakes pointing at each other. The chip the tool understood the least got the better score.
The fix for the regex was one token. Let the PRO badge be optional, and make the tier digit optional too so the pattern is more forgiving about names it hasn’t seen. The model number still has to be four digits, which keeps a Threadripper’s “3970X” from getting read as a generation-3 desktop part.
The deeper fix was the generation math itself, the thing that had the 5800X on a 0.4 to begin with. AMD’s series numbers don’t line up with Intel’s generation count, so I wrote out the mapping by hand: 5000-series is Zen 3 from late 2020, which competes with Intel’s 11th gen, and so on down the list. Once both the name parsing and the generation mapping were fixed, the 5850U and the 5800X finally sorted the right way around, desktop on top.
I pinned it with a test I should have written first: for a given tier, a newer Ryzen has to be worth at least as much as an older one. Zen 5 not less than Zen 4, Zen 4 not less than Zen 3. It is a weak assertion. It doesn’t check that the dollar amount is right, only that the ordering isn’t insane. But the whole bug was an ordering that was insane, so it is the assertion that would have caught it.
What I’d do differently: I built this by pattern-matching on names, one regex at a time, adding cases as I ran into machines that broke it. That works right up until two chips have names shaped differently enough that one matches and one doesn’t, and then you get a laptop worth more than a desktop. If I started over I’d price against a small reference table of real chips with known rough values and treat the parser’s job as “find the closest row,” instead of “compute a number from the string.” Names lie about hardware in a hundred small ways. A table at least lies consistently.
Still a hobby script. It’s closer to right than it was. That’s the most I’ll claim.
https://github.com/TiltedLunar123/pc-worth
